The Benefit of Plant-Made Pharmaceuticals
=========================================

The Rising Star of Healthcare: Plant-Made Pharmaceuticals
---------------------------------------------------------

The pharmaceutical industry is witnessing a revolutionary shift towards greener, more sustainable practices with the advent of plant-based medicines. This innovative approach to drug production not only promises to transform the way we make medicines, but also offers a beacon of hope for addressing global health challenges.

Understanding Plant-Made Pharmaceuticals
----------------------------------------

At its core, plant-based medicine is the use of genetically modified plants to produce proteins and compounds that can be used as medicines. This field, often referred to as “pharming”, combines modern biotechnology with cutting-edge horticultural practices to create a new era of drug production. In particular, [BionFarming](https://bionfarming.com/innovations)has mastered the combination of state-of-the-art plant cultivation, specialised bioreactor cultivation and plant-specific genetic engineering to deliver the best results in new therapies for its customers.

![Image 3](https://mletkuocru8e.i.optimole.com/w:825/h:1024/q:mauto/ig:avif/https://bionfarming.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Pflanze.png)

##### BIONFARMING

Scaling like nature
-------------------

BionFarm are designed to be grouped together for greater performance. Connecting two or more BionSpheres together creates a network effect and with significant economies of scale. The BionFarm can be manufactures economically in large numbers, applying the highly efficient methods used in automotive production. In this way, plant growth can be optimised to meet any demand

### The Advantages of Plant-Based Production

The sustainability of plant-based pharmaceuticals cannot be overstated. With the ability to produce large quantities of medicine with minimal environmental impact, the process is efficient and environmentally friendly. In addition, the scalability of plant-based drug production allows for rapid response to health emergencies, such as pandemic outbreaks.

#### Innovations in Plant-Made Pharmaceuticals

Recent years have seen significant breakthroughs in this field, with the development of vaccines, antibodies and therapeutic proteins using plant-based systems. These advances highlight the potential for plants to become the factories of the future for a wide range of pharmaceuticals.

The Environmental Impact
------------------------

One of the most compelling arguments for plant-based pharmaceuticals is their reduced carbon footprint. By harnessing photosynthesis, this approach offers a cleaner, more sustainable alternative to traditional drug manufacturing processes, which are often energy-intensive and polluting. Above all, the new biotechnology is also enabling other industries, from pharmaceuticals to [mining](https://bionfarming.com/industrial-applications/), to become more sustainable, because in these industries the excellent products from plants act as a driver for sustainability.

### Reducing Carbon Footprint

Plant-based pharmaceutical production significantly reduces greenhouse gas emissions, contributing to the fight against climate change. In addition, the conservation of resources such as water and land underlines the environmental benefits of this innovative process.

Health benefits
---------------

In addition to their environmental and economic advantages, plant-based pharmaceuticals offer several health benefits. These drugs are often more natural and have fewer side effects than their synthetic counterparts, making them a safer choice for patients.

### Accessibility and availability

A key advantage of plant-based medicines is their potential to improve global access to medicines. By making medicines more affordable and easier to produce, this technology could play a crucial role in improving healthcare in developing countries.

Economic impact
---------------

The economic benefits of plant-based medicines go beyond cost savings for consumers. For manufacturers, the lower capital and operating costs associated with the production of plant-based medicines can lead to increased profitability and market competitiveness. This economic model not only makes healthcare more accessible, but also encourages investment in research and development in the sector.

### Cost-effectiveness for consumers

One of the most immediate benefits for patients is the potential reduction in the cost of medicines. Due to the way they are produced, plant-based medicines can be more affordable than traditional medicines, making essential medicines available to a wider population.

### Impact on the pharmaceutical industry

The introduction of plant-made medicines has the potential to revolutionise the pharmaceutical industry. This shift towards more sustainable and efficient production methods could encourage innovation, reduce costs and lead to the development of new medicines that may not have been possible using traditional approaches.

#### Future directions

The future of plant-based pharmaceuticals is bright, with endless opportunities for growth and innovation. However, realising this potential will require overcoming certain challenges, such as the regulatory hurdles and ethical considerations associated with genetic engineering and the production of plant-based medicines.

### Research and development

Continued investment in research and development is essential to expand the range of plant-based medicines. This will not only improve the efficacy of existing drugs, but also lead to the discovery of new treatments for a wide range of diseases.

#### Regulatory and ethical considerations

As the field of plant-based medicines develops, it will be important to navigate the complex regulatory landscape to ensure that these medicines are safe, effective and ethically produced. The establishment of clear guidelines and standards will be critical to the widespread adoption of this technology.

### Conclusion

The benefits of plant-made pharmaceuticals are many and offer a promising alternative to traditional drug production methods. With benefits ranging from environmental sustainability to improved access to medicines, this innovative approach has the potential to transform the pharmaceutical industry and have a significant impact on global healthcare. As research and technology continue to advance, the future of plant-made pharmaceuticals looks increasingly promising, heralding a new era of medicine that is greener, more efficient and more accessible to all.

PMCID: PMC9931573 PMID: [36820221](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36820221/)

Abstract
--------

The production of pharmaceutical compounds in plants is attracting increasing attention, as plant-based systems can be less expensive, safer, and more scalable than mammalian, yeast, bacterial, and insect cell expression systems. Here, we review the history and current status of plant-made pharmaceuticals. Producing pharmaceuticals in plants requires pairing the appropriate plant species with suitable transformation technology. Pharmaceuticals have been produced in tobacco, cereals, legumes, fruits, and vegetables via nuclear transformation, chloroplast transformation, transient expression, and transformation of suspension cell cultures. Despite this wide range of species and methods used, most such efforts have involved the nuclear transformation of tobacco. Tobacco readily generates large amounts of biomass, easily accepts foreign genes, and is amenable to stable gene expression via nuclear transformation. Although vaccines, antibodies, and therapeutic proteins have been produced in plants, such pharmaceuticals are not readily utilized by humans due to differences in glycosylation, and few such compounds have been approved due to a lack of clinical data. In addition, achieving an adequate immune response using plant-made pharmaceuticals can be difficult due to low rates of production compared to other expression systems. Various technologies have recently been developed to help overcome these limitations; however, plant systems are expected to increasingly become widely used expression systems for recombinant protein production.

**Keywords:** Biopharmaceuticals, Plant production system, Plant-made pharmaceuticals, Molecular farming, Recombinant protein, Transgenic plant, Transient expression

Introduction
------------

Recombinant therapeutic proteins are transgenic proteins that are produced in a heterologous organism and are used to prevent or treat a human or animal disease. The first commercially available recombinant therapeutic protein was human insulin produced in _Escherichia coli_ (Goeddel et al. [1979](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR41)). Since then, as the technology has developed, other therapeutics, vaccines, and cytokines have been produced to protect people from infectious diseases and as cures for previously incurable diseases (Meyer et al. [2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR84)). Unlike traditional pharmaceuticals produced by chemical synthesis, recombinant proteins are larger, with more complex structures. For example, aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) is composed of 21 atoms, whereas a monoclonal antibody used as an anticancer agent is composed of 25,000 atoms and exhibits a complex structure (Otto et al. [2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR92)). Given this complexity, systems to produce such recombinant proteins generally use living cells, including _E. coli_, yeast, insect, and mammalian cells (Thomas et al. [2002](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR131)).

The production system is chosen based on the type of drug being produced. Prokaryotic production systems are used to produce proteins that have simple structures and lack post-translational modifications such as N-glycosylation, whereas eukaryotic production systems are used to produce proteins that have complex structures (such as antibodies) and post-translational modifications (Ferrer-Miralles et al. [2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR30)). Bacterial expression systems were the first systems utilized to produce recombinant proteins, and they remain in use because bacterial cells are easy to handle and grow rapidly (Redwan [2007](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR104)). However, bacterial systems also have significant disadvantages, including the presence of endotoxin and the production of proteins lacking glycosylation. For these reasons, and because most biopharmaceuticals require post-translational protein processing for function, eukaryotic yeast and mammalian cell expression systems have accounted for the majority of biopharmaceutical production (Butler [2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR9)). Like bacteria, yeast cells are inexpensive to produce and grow quickly. In addition, they can produce glycosylated proteins (Gellison et al. [1992](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR38); Choi et al. [2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR11); Demain and Vaishnav [2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR22)). Yeast cell disruption is difficult, however, due to the presence of the cell wall (Çelik and Çalık [2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR10)). Recombinant proteins produced in insect cells can undergo proper folding and post-translational modification; however, insect cell systems are expensive and time-consuming to use (Fernandes et al. [2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR29)). Because the mammalian cell culture system has the same advantages as the insect cell system and is optimized for human protein production, most recombinant protein production platforms are based on mammalian cells. Such systems have been approved for the production of a number of drugs (O'Flaherty et al. [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR90)). However, these systems are highly vulnerable to infection by animal pathogens, are difficult to scale up and require expensive culture conditions (Butler [2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR9)).

To overcome these disadvantages, plant production systems for biopharmaceuticals have been developed (Fig.[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#Fig1)) (Shanmugaraj et al. [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR116)). Plants are generally suitable for recombinant protein production. Recombinant proteins can be rapidly produced in plants via transient expression (Kapila et al. [1997](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR59)). In addition, plant production systems cannot be infected by mammalian pathogens during protein production and are easy to scale up (Yusibov and Rabindran [2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR142)). The production cost when using plant production system is estimated to be only 0.1% and 2–10% that when using mammalian cell culture and microbial fermentation systems, respectively (Giddings [2001](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR40)). Moreover, their culture conditions are simple, and since plants are eukaryotic, the recombinant proteins undergo post-translational modification (Fischer et al. [1999a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR32)). However, the glycosylation process in plants is different from that in animal cell systems (Bosch et al. [2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR6)). Although few plant-made recombinant proteins have been approved to date for use as therapeutic agents, these problems are being resolved with advances in biotechnology (Schillberg et al. [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR113)). The production of biopharmaceuticals in plants—known as molecular farming, a term introduced by Fischer et al. ([1999a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR32), [b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR33))—offers benefits such as scalability, speed, and improved safety compared with insect and mammalian cell expression systems. These features are desirable when resources are limited and could be helpful for the rapid production of vaccines against pandemic disease outbreaks or biochemical terrorism (Fischer et al. [1999a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR32), [b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR33)).

### Fig. 1.

[![Image 9: Fig. 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/9740/9931573/34121a450009/11816_2023_821_Fig1_HTML.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=9931573_11816_2023_821_Fig1_HTML.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/figure/Fig1/)

Schematic diagram of plant-made pharmaceuticals production

The US Food and Drug Administration approved the first vaccine produced in _Nicotiana tabacum_ cell suspension culture expression system in 2006; this vaccine protects Newcastle disease virus in poultry (Vermij and Waltz [2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR136)). In 2012, the first plant-made pharmaceutical (PMP) for humans was approved: a recombinant human glucocerebrosidase produced in a carrot (_Daucus carota_) cell suspension culture system. This enzyme is used to treat Gaucher’s disease, a hereditary lysosomal storage disorder caused by a mutation of the _β-glucocerebrosidase_ gene (Fox [2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR36); Rosales-Mendoza and Tello-Olea [2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR108)). The disease is characterized by enlargement of the liver and spleen, fatigue, and anemia (Tekoah et al. [2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR129)). Since then, PMPs have been developed using various technologies, and the demand for these products is growing. The US Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA), which is interested in the potential use of plant-derived vaccine production technology, has invested in both proof-of-concept operations and the development of commercial-scale facilities for plant-derived vaccine manufacturing. In particular, DARPA announced the design and operation of a facility that can produce tens of millions of doses of plant-derived vaccines via transient expression in _Nicotiana benthamiana_ (Holtz et al. [2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR50))_._ These vaccines can be ready for use within 1 month after the plants are treated with an appropriate vector, showing how quickly the production of “green vaccines” can be established using transient expression. By emphasizing how easily the upstream process could be expanded, the authors demonstrated the suitability of plant-derived vaccine production as a means to rapidly protect the population against new infectious diseases.

In this review, we discuss pharmaceutical proteins produced in plants. First, we look at the types of recombinant pharmaceutical proteins developed so far, including vaccines, antibodies, and therapeutic proteins. Next, we discuss tools and technologies for producing these proteins in plants, including production methods in various plant systems. Finally, we discuss the limitations of recombinant protein production using plant systems compared to mammalian, insect, yeast, and _E. coli_ systems, as well as the current state of research and development aimed at solving these problems.

Three types of PMPs
-------------------

High-value-added PMPs produced from plants include vaccines, antibodies, and therapeutic proteins. Clinical trials have been conducted on various PMPs, which are listed in Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#Tab1) ([https://clinicaltrials.gov](https://clinicaltrials.gov/), accessed on December 2022).

### Table 1.

Plant-made pharmaceuticals in clinical trials

| Product | Expression platform | Disease/pathogen | Clinical trial status | Company | ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P2G12 antibody | _Nicotiana tabacum_ | Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) | Phase I completed (2011) | University of Surrey, Guildford, UK | [NCT01403792](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01403792) |
| HAI-05 vaccine | _Nicotiana benthamiana_ | H5N1 | Phase I completed (2011) | Fraunhofer, Center for Molecular Biotechnology, USA | [NCT01250795](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01250795) |
| Plant cell-expressed recombinant glucocerebrosidase (prGCD) | Carrot cell culture | Gaucher's disease | Phase III completed and FDA approved (2012) | Protalix, Karmiel, Israel | [NCT00376168](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT00376168) |
| H5-VLP + GLA-AF vaccine | _Nicotiana benthamiana_ | H5N1 | Phase I completed (2014) | Infectious Disease Research Institute, Seattle, WA, USA | [NCT01657929](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01657929) |
| Autologous FL vaccine | _Nicotiana benthamiana_ | Lymphoma follicular | Phase I completed (2015) | Icon Genetics, Munchen, Germany | [NCT01022255](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01022255) |
| Pfs25 VLP-FhCMB | _Nicotiana benthamiana_ | Malaria | Phase I completed (2015) | Center for Molecular Biotechnology, Plymouth, MI, USA | [NCT02013687](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02013687) |
| PA83-FhCMB | _Nicotiana benthamiana_ | Anthrax | Phase I completed (2015) | Center for Molecular Biotechnology, Plymouth, MI, USA | [NCT02239172](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02239172) |
| PRX-102 | Tobacco cell culture (BY2 Cell) | Fabry disease | Phase II completed (2016) | Protalix, Karmiel, Israel | NCT01769001 |
| ZMApp | _Nicotiana benthamiana_ | Ebola virus | Phase II completed (2017) | National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Disease (NIAID), Bethesda, MD, USA | [NCT02363322](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT02363322) |
| CP-PRO-CoVLP-024 | _Nicotiana benthamiana_ | COVID-19 | Approved (Canada, 2022) | Medicago, Quebec, QC, Canada | [NCT05040789](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT05040789) |
| KBP-201 | _Nicotiana benthamiana_ | COVID-19 | Phase II (2021) | Kentucky BioProcessing, Owensboro, KY, USA | [NCT04473690](https://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04473690) |

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/table/Tab1/)

### Vaccines

Vaccines confer acquired immunity against specific diseases or pathogens in animals, including humans. Vaccines are similar in structure to the antigen recognition sites of microbial pathogens that cause disease, but unlike pathogens, they are not pathogenic. Thus, vaccines activate the immune system, allowing it to quickly respond to subsequent infections with the same pathogen. The demand for plant-based vaccines has continuously increasing since the development of a vaccine against Newcastle virus in 2006 (Yusibov and Rabindran [2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR142); Shim et al. [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR118)). Research on various pathogens is being actively conducted, and some vaccines are currently being evaluated for safety and efficacy. In addition to general vaccines, which have attenuated pathogenicity, other types of vaccines are available, such as those made from inactivated viruses, recombinant protein subunits, and nucleic acids (Ellis [1999](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR27)). In particular, a vaccine made from virus-like particles (VLPs; a type of recombinant protein subunit) is attracting attention (Noad and Roy [2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR88)).

A VLP that is similar to a virus shape is not infectious because it does not contain viral genetic materials. Although VLPs lack pathogenicity, they contain the viral antigen(s) found on the outside of the capsid and are immunogenic (Murray [1988](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR86); Pumpens and Grens [2002](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR102)). Representative plant-produced vaccines include vaccines against the hepatitis B virus (Huang et al. [2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR52)), Newcastle disease virus (Guerrero-Andrade et al. [2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR42)), influenza (D’Aoust et al. [2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR15); Kalthoff et al. [2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR57); Kanagarajan et al. [2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR58); Shoji et al. [2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR119); Firsov et al. [2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR31); Mbewana et al. [2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR82); Smith et al. [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR121)), porcine circovirus (Gunter et al. [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR43)), Zika virus (Yang et al. [2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR141)), and poliovirus (Marsian et al. [2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR80)). Recently, two types of plant-made COVID-19 vaccines were rapidly developed. Medicago's coronavirus VLP (CoVLP) is a vaccine that mimics the surface structure of the SARS-CoV-2 virus. Using a transient expression system in _N. benthamiana_, after the nucleotide sequence of the virus is obtained, a vaccine can be successfully produced within 8 weeks (Nosaki et al. [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR89)). This process is four- to eightfold faster than the currently used egg-production system for the influenza vaccine. Based on the promising results of the phase 1 clinical trial, phase 2/3 clinical trials were conducted and it was approved in Canada in 2022 (Ward et al. [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR139); [https://www.canada.ca/en/services/drugs-health-products.html](https://www.canada.ca/en/services/drugs-health-products.html), accessed on December 2022). Kentucky Bioprocessing (KBP) also produced a VLP-based vaccine in _N. benthamiana_. This vaccine candidate, KBP-201, is a chimeric VLP (cVLP) comprising the expressed SARS-CoV-2 receptor-binding domain (RBD) and a modified tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) (Maharjan et al. [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR76)). Since cVLPs consist of viruses that are not related to the antigens, they function as heterologous antigens; such molecules can induce a robust antibody response (Kushnir et al. [2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR64)).

### Antibodies

Antibodies are substances produced by the immune system in response to stimulation by antigens. Their basic structure comprises two light chains and two heavy chains, which form a Y shape through disulfide bonds. The amino acid sequences of antibodies vary depending on the type of antigen, allowing countless types of antibodies to be produced. In 1989, the production of single gamma or kappa immunoglobulin chains in tobacco was made possible for the first time (Hiatt et al. [1989](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR47)); since then, numerous plant-made antibody therapeutics have been researched and developed. The plant-produced antibody drug ZMapp was developed by Mapp Pharmaceutical to treat Ebola virus (Pettitt et al. [2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR97)). In addition, antibodies against cancer (So et al. [2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR122); Rattanapisit et al. [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR103)), human immunodeficiency virus (Floss et al. [2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR35); Sainsbury and Lomonossoff [2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR109); Holland et al. [2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR49)), Zika, herpes (Diamos et al. [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR24)), and dengue (Dent et al. [2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR23)) are currently being developed.

### Therapeutic proteins

Cytokines are small glycoproteins involved in a variety of processes in the cell, including cell proliferation, differentiation, and migration. As cytokines are components of the human immune system, their most widely known role is to mediate signal transduction between adjacent or distant cells, causing positive or negative feedback cascades (Arai et al. [1990](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR3)). The more than 100 cytokines identified to date are divided into several families, including hematopoietins, interferons, platelet-derived growth factors, tumor necrosis factors, tumor growth factors, and chemokines (Liles and Van Voorhis [1995](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR74)). Cytokines can also be produced in animal cells, but due to the potential for contamination, efforts are focused on producing these molecules in plant systems. Using a coat protein (CP)-deficient bamboo mosaic virus (BaMV) vector, the expression level of human interferon-γ was higher compared to that of other viral vectors in _N. benthamiana_ (Jiang et al. [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR55)). Moreover, the efficient removal of the affinity tag after purification during the production of human interleukin-6 reduced production costs.

The bleeding disorder hemophilia affects a small percentage of the world’s population, but it imposes a great burden on those who have it. In particular, more than half of hemophilia patients do not receive adequate treatment. Hemophilia is caused by a deficiency of factor VIII (FVIII), a blood coagulation factor (Mannucci and Tuddenham [2001](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR77)). Because FVIII is known to undergo complex post-translational modification, research has focused on producing a therapeutic agent in eukaryotes. In plants, the FVIII domain was produced in _N. tabacum_ chloroplasts and bioencapsulated in plant cells up to 0.4 mg/g in fresh leaves (Verma et al. [2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR135); Sherman et al. [2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR117)). Furthermore, the entire FVIII factor was produced in the chloroplasts of lettuce (_Lactuca sativa_) at a level of 852 µg/g (Kwon et al. [2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR68)). Another blood coagulation factor, a serine protease a-thrombin precursor (pFIIa) involved in fibrin formation, was co-expressed in _N. benthamiana_ with Turnip Crinkle Virus coat protein (TCV-CP), which is known to interfere with post-transcriptional gene silencing (Laguia-Becher et al. [2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR69)).

Human serum albumin (HSA), an abundant protein in the human body, accounts for more than half of the total protein content in plasma. Due to its strong binding properties, HSA acts as a carrier for hormones and metabolites and is also used to treat diseases or injuries such as hypovolemia, burns, and bleeding. HSA can be extracted from blood, but there has been considerable effort made to diversify its production due to the limited availability of donor human blood and the potential for blood-based infection. Studies to produce HSA using tobacco and rice (_Oryza sativa_) plants have been attempted (He et al. [2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR45)). HSA expression in rice was successfully increased by knock-in of an HSA expression cassette at the locus encoding GluA1, a rice storage protein (Sedaghati et al. [2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR115)).

Tools and techniques for PMP expression
---------------------------------------

Two major methods are used to produce recombinant proteins in plant systems: stable genetic transformation and transient gene expression (Table [2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#Tab2)) (Potrykus [1991](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR101); Paul and Ma [2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9931573/#CR96)). enriched_points.expanded_content: Plant-made pharmaceuticals offer cost-effective production, environmental sustainability, and enhanced safety, making them a viable alternative to traditional pharmaceuticals, potentially revolutionizing global healthcare access.

Increasing public and professional knowledge about plant-based pharmaceuticals is crucial for acceptance, ensuring stakeholders understand their benefits, applications, and the science behind this innovative approach.

Integrating plant-made pharmaceuticals into healthcare can lead to personalized medicine, rapid vaccine production, and improved disease management, fostering a more resilient and sustainable healthcare system.
